content:
First, observe that for \(n<17\) one has
\[
n^2-15n-27<0,
\]
while the product of the digits of a positive integer is always \(\ge0\).  Hence no solutions occur for \(n\le16\).  For \(n\ge17\) we must have

 product of digits \(\le9^d\), where \(d\) is the number of digits of \(n\),  
 but  
 \[
n^2-15n-27>9^d
\]
as soon as \(n\) exceeds a small bound depending on \(d\).  In fact:

- If \(n\) has two digits then its digit–product \(\le81\).  But
  \[
  n^2-15n-27\le81\iff n\le20.316,
  \]
  so we need only check \(n=17,18,19,20\).  A quick check shows only
  \(n=17\) works:
  \[
    17^2-15\cdot17-27=289-255-27=7,\quad1\cdot7=7.
  \]
- For three (or more) digits, \(n\ge100\) makes \(n^2-15n-27\) exceed the maximum possible digit–product (\(9^3=729\), \(9^4=6561\), …).  Hence no larger solutions.

Thus the only solution is \(n=17\), and the sum of all such \(n\) is

\[
\boxed{17}.
\]

\[

 product of digits \(\le9^d\), where \(d\) is the number of digits of \(n\),

 \[

as soon as \(n\) exceeds a small bound depending on \(d\).  In fact:

  n^2-15n-27\le81\iff n\le20.316,

  \[
